title: The Single-Attention Limit
concept: A single self-attention head averages relationships, merging different types of connections (e.g. grammar vs subject-object) into one blurry score.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of self-attention in an AI language model?

options.text: To find connections and relationships between words in a sentence.
To delete words that are too long or difficult.
To check the spelling of every word.

exercises.explain: Self-attention helps AI models find connections between different words in a sentence to understand context.

cards.title: Too Many Voices
cards.body: Have you ever tried to listen to two people talking to you at the very same time? You end up hearing a messy mix of both conversations. A single attention head in an AI faces the exact same problem when trying to understand a sentence!

cards.title: The Single-Attention Limit
cards.body: The Single-Attention Limit is the restriction where one self-attention head can only create one map of connections at a time. Because it only has one map, it has to average out different kinds of relationships, blending them into a single blurry score.

cards.title: The Highlighter Mix-Up
cards.body: Imagine using a single colored marker to highlight a textbook. If you highlight both grammar rules and vocabulary words in yellow, they all blend together. You cannot easily tell which highlighted word is a verb and which is a key vocabulary term because they are all colored the same way.

cards.title: A Blurry Recipe
cards.body: In the sentence "The chef cooked the soup," we have a grammar connection (chef -> cooked) and a subject-object connection (chef -> soup). A single attention head tries to track both but ends up averaging them, leaving the AI confused about who did what to whom.

cards.title: Can One Head Do It All?
cards.body: People often think that making a single attention head larger or more powerful allows it to track multiple independent relationships at once. In reality, no matter how big it is, one head can still only produce one blended map. To see different relationships clearly, we need multiple heads working together.

cards.title: One Map vs. Many Maps
cards.body: A single head has to squish all connections into one map, while multiple heads can look at different things at the same time.

right.text: Keeps grammar and meaning in separate, clean maps.

left.text: Averages grammar and meaning into one blurry, messy score.

exercises.prompt: What happens when a single self-attention head tries to handle both grammar rules and subject-object relationships?

options.text: It ignores all verbs in the sentence.
It merges different relationships into a single blurry score.
It instantly translates the sentence into another language.
It separates nouns and adjectives into different files.

exercises.explain: Because a single attention head has to represent everything in one map, different linguistic relationships get mixed together, resulting in a blurry average.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A single self-attention head is forced to average different connections because it can only create one attention map at a time.
exercises.explain: A single attention head can only build one attention map, which forces it to average different relationship types together.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how a single attention head processes multiple connection types.
exercises.sentence: Instead of keeping grammar and meaning separate, a single attention head connects them by taking their factors and creating blurry inputs because it ________ the relationships.
exercises.explain: A single attention head cannot keep connections separate; instead, it averages them together, which makes the relationships look blurry to the AI.

exercises.prompt: Which of these shows an AI suffering from the Single-Attention Limit on the sentence "The chef cooked the soup"?

options.text: Correctly identifies that the chef is the person who did the cooking.
Gets confused and averages the words so it thinks the soup cooked the chef.
Correctly links the action to the chef and the target to the soup.

exercises.explain: The AI output with a blurry, mixed-up understanding of who did what represents the limitation of a single attention head averaging connections. The correct outputs show distinct, clear understandings.

exercises.prompt: Why is a single self-attention head unable to perfectly track both grammar rules and subject-object relationships at the same time?

options.text: It runs out of computer memory and crashes the entire AI system.
It forces the model to only look at the very first word of the sentence.
It averages different relationships together, creating a blurry, mixed-up connection score.
It completely ignores nouns and only focuses on punctuation marks.

exercises.explain: A single attention head is limited to a single attention map, forcing it to average out different types of connections (like grammar rules and factual relationships) into one blurry blend. Multiple heads solve this by specializing.
